A [general election](/wiki/United_Kingdom_general_elections_overview "United Kingdom general elections overview") was held in the United Kingdom on Thursday 18 June 1970. It resulted in a surprise victory for the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") under leader [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath"), which defeated the governing [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") under Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson"). The [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)"), under its new leader [Jeremy Thorpe](/wiki/Jeremy_Thorpe "Jeremy Thorpe"), lost half its seats. The Conservatives, including the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") (UUP), secured a majority of 30 seats. This general election was the first in which people could vote from the age of 18, after passage of the [Representation of the People Act](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1969 "Representation of the People Act 1969") the previous year, and the first UK election in which party affiliations of candidates were put on the ballots.[[1]](#cite_note-2)

Most opinion polls prior to the election indicated a comfortable Labour victory, and put Labour up to 12.4% ahead of the Conservatives. On election day, however, a late swing gave the Conservatives a 3.4% lead and ended almost six years of Labour government, although Wilson remained leader of the Labour Party in opposition. Writing in the aftermath of the election, the political scientist [Richard Rose](/wiki/Richard_Rose_(political_scientist) "Richard Rose (political scientist)") described the Conservative victory as "surprising" and noted a significant shift in votes between the two main parties.[[2]](#cite_note-RoseTimes-3) *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* journalist George Clark wrote that the election would be "remembered as the occasion when the people of the United Kingdom hurled the findings of the opinion polls back into the faces of the pollsters".[[3]](#cite_note-ClarkTimes-4)

The result would provide the mandate for Heath as Prime Minister to begin formal negotiations for the United Kingdom to become a member state of the [European Communities](/wiki/European_Communities "European Communities") (EC)—or the "Common Market" as it was more widely known at the time, before it later became the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"); the [UK officially joined the EC](/wiki/Accession_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_the_European_Communities "Accession of the United Kingdom to the European Communities") on 1 January 1973, along with the Republic of Ireland and Denmark.

Frontbench Labour politicians [George Brown](/wiki/George_Brown_(British_Labour_politician) "George Brown (British Labour politician)") and [Jennie Lee](/wiki/Jennie_Lee,_Baroness_Lee_of_Asheridge "Jennie Lee, Baroness Lee of Asheridge") were voted out at this election.

This marked the end of a series of elections where both main parties won over 40% of the vote. This would not occur again for the Conservatives for [nine years](/wiki/1979_United_Kingdom_general_election "1979 United Kingdom general election"); Labour would wait [27](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election").

The result was cast as a two-party politics outcome, with no third party reaching 10% of the (total) vote. Such an outcome would not happen again until [the 2017 election](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election").

The election was the last in which a nationwide UK party gained seats in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").[[4]](#cite_note-5) The UUP sat with the Conservative Party at [Westminster](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom"), traditionally taking the Conservative parliamentary [whip](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)"). To all intents and purposes the UUP functioned as the Northern Ireland branch of the Conservative Party. However, hardline unionist [Ian Paisley](/wiki/Ian_Paisley "Ian Paisley") unseated the UUP incumbent in [North Antrim](/wiki/North_Antrim_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Antrim (UK Parliament constituency)"), a clear sign that the UUP's complete dominance over unionist politics in Northern Ireland was already starting to weaken. In 1972, in protest over the permanent prorogation of the [Parliament of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Northern_Ireland "Parliament of Northern Ireland"), Westminster UUP MPs withdrew from the alliance.[[5]](#cite_note-6)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Election date

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Election date")]

The date of 18 June was supposedly chosen because [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") wanted as [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") to go to the polls before the [introduction of decimal coinage](/wiki/Decimal_Day "Decimal Day") in early 1971, for which his government had been responsible and which he thought was hugely unpopular,[[6]](#cite_note-Joe_Haines_2003-7)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] and because Wilson sought to gain some momentum by surprising the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)"), who were expecting an October election.[[6]](#cite_note-Joe_Haines_2003-7)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overview")]

Commentators believed that an unexpectedly bad set of balance of payments figures (a £31-million trade deficit) published three days before the election and a loss of national prestige after the England football team's defeat by West Germany on 14 June in the [World Cup](/wiki/1970_FIFA_World_Cup "1970 FIFA World Cup") contributed to the [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") defeat.[[7]](#cite_note-SusVic-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Iain_Macleod.jpg/250px-Iain_Macleod.jpg)](/wiki/File:Iain_Macleod.jpg)

Shadow Chancellor [Iain Macleod](/wiki/Iain_Macleod "Iain Macleod") campaigning in [York](/wiki/York "York").

Other factors that were cited as reasons for the Conservative victory included union indiscipline, rising prices, the risk of devaluation, the imposition of [Selective Employment Tax](/wiki/Selective_Employment_Tax "Selective Employment Tax") (SET), and a set of jobless figures released on final week of the campaign showing unemployment at its highest level since 1940. Interviewed by [Robin Day](/wiki/Robin_Day "Robin Day"), the outgoing Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson") highlighted the possibility that "complacency engendered by the opinion polls" may have resulted in a poor turnout of Labour supporters.[[8]](#cite_note-BBC_Election_Results_Programme_1970-9)

As [defending world champions](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"), England's venture in the World Cup attracted a much keener public interest than the general election did.[[9]](#cite_note-10) However an analysis by pollster [Matt Singh](/wiki/Matt_Singh "Matt Singh") for the 50th anniversary of the election concluded that the late swing had been caused by the weak economic data and that there was "no evidence" that the World Cup had influenced the outcome.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

American pollster Douglas Schoen and [Oxford University](/wiki/Oxford_University "Oxford University") academic [R. W. Johnson](/wiki/R._W._Johnson "R. W. Johnson") asserted that [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell") had attracted 2.5 million votes to the Conservatives, although the Conservative vote only increased by 1.7 million. Johnson later stated "It became clear that Powell had won the 1970 election for the Tories ... of all those who had switched their vote from one party to another, 50 per cent were working class Powellites".[[11]](#cite_note-12) The Professor of Political Science [Randall Hansen](/wiki/Randall_Hansen "Randall Hansen") assessed a range of studies, including some which contended that Powell had made little or no difference to the result, but concluded that "At the very least, Powell's effect was likely to have fired up the Conservative vote in constituencies which would have voted Tory in any event".[[12]](#cite_note-13)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Election night commentators [Michael Barratt](/wiki/Michael_Barratt_(television_presenter) "Michael Barratt (television presenter)") and Jeffrey Preece dismissed any special "Powell factor", as did Conservative MPs [Reginald Maudling](/wiki/Reginald_Maudling "Reginald Maudling"), [Timothy Raison](/wiki/Timothy_Raison "Timothy Raison") and [Hugh Dykes](/wiki/Hugh_Dykes "Hugh Dykes").[[8]](#cite_note-BBC_Election_Results_Programme_1970-9)

The 1970–74 Parliament has to date been the only time since the 1924–29 Parliament in which the Conservative Party were only in government for one term before returning to opposition.

The most notable casualty of the election was [George Brown](/wiki/George_Brown_(British_Labour_politician) "George Brown (British Labour politician)"), deputy leader of the Labour Party, who lost to the Conservative candidate in the [Belper](/wiki/Belper_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Belper (UK Parliament constituency)") constituency. Brown had held the seat since 1945. Labour [Minister for the Arts](/wiki/Parliamentary_Under-Secretary_of_State_for_Arts_and_Heritage "Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Arts and Heritage"), [Jennie Lee](/wiki/Jennie_Lee,_Baroness_Lee_of_Asheridge "Jennie Lee, Baroness Lee of Asheridge") lost her [Cannock](/wiki/Cannock_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Cannock (UK Parliament constituency)") seat, held by Labour since [1935](/wiki/1935_United_Kingdom_general_election "1935 United Kingdom general election") on a swing of 10.7% to the Conservatives in what [Richard Rose](/wiki/Richard_Rose_(political_scientist) "Richard Rose (political scientist)") called "the biggest upset" of the election.[[13]](#cite_note-TimesGuide1970Rose-14)

Unusually for the [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(UK) "Liberal Party (UK)"), the by-elections between 1966 and 1970 had proved almost fruitless, with many Liberal candidates losing deposits. The one exception was its [by-election gain of Birmingham Ladywood](/wiki/1969_Birmingham_Ladywood_by-election "1969 Birmingham Ladywood by-election") in June 1969; this was promptly lost in the 1970 general election. The party found itself struggling to introduce its new leader [Jeremy Thorpe](/wiki/Jeremy_Thorpe "Jeremy Thorpe") to the public, owing to the extensive coverage and attention paid to [Enoch Powell](/wiki/Enoch_Powell "Enoch Powell"). The election result was poor for the Liberals, with Thorpe only narrowly winning his own seat in [North Devon](/wiki/North_Devon "North Devon").[[7]](#cite_note-SusVic-8) Indeed, of the six MPs returned, three (Thorpe, [David Steel](/wiki/David_Steel "David Steel") and [John Pardoe](/wiki/John_Pardoe "John Pardoe")) were elected by a majority of less than 1,000 votes.[[14]](#cite_note-TimesGuide1970p250-15)

The BBC's election coverage was led by [Cliff Michelmore](/wiki/Cliff_Michelmore "Cliff Michelmore"), along with [Robin Day](/wiki/Robin_Day "Robin Day"), [David Butler](/wiki/David_Butler_(psephologist) "David Butler (psephologist)") and [Robert McKenzie](/wiki/Robert_McKenzie_(psephologist) "Robert McKenzie (psephologist)").[[7]](#cite_note-SusVic-8) There were periodic cutaways to the BBC regions. This coverage has been rerun on [BBC Parliament](/wiki/BBC_Parliament "BBC Parliament") on several occasions, including on 18 July 2005 as a tribute to [Edward Heath](/wiki/Edward_Heath "Edward Heath") after his death the previous day. Its most recent screening was on the 20th of June 2020, to commemorate the 50th anniversary of its first transmission.[[15]](#cite_note-16) The BBC coverage was parodied by *[Monty Python's Flying Circus](/wiki/Monty_Python%27s_Flying_Circus "Monty Python's Flying Circus")* in its famous "[Election Night Special](/wiki/Election_Night_Special "Election Night Special")" sketch.

Both BBC and [ITN](/wiki/ITN "ITN") carried their 1970 election night broadcasts in colour, although segments broadcast from some remote locations and some BBC and ITN regional bureaus were transmitted in black-and-white. Some [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)") regions were not yet broadcasting in colour at the time of the 1970 elections.

The [right to vote](/wiki/Right_to_vote "Right to vote") in this election was widened by the Labour government's [Sixth Reform Act](/wiki/Sixth_Reform_Act "Sixth Reform Act"), which reduced the [voting age](/wiki/Voting_age "Voting age") from 21 to 18 years. The United Kingdom was the first major democratic nation to extend suffrage to this age group.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19) Case law subsequently established the right for [undergraduate students](/wiki/Undergraduate_students "Undergraduate students") to vote in the constituency of their university. This followed an appeal to the [High Court](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice").[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Timeline")]

The Prime Minister, [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson"), visited [Buckingham Palace](/wiki/Buckingham_Palace "Buckingham Palace") on 18 May and asked the Queen to [dissolve Parliament](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Dissolution of the Parliament of the United Kingdom") on 29 May, announcing that the election would be held on 18 June. The key dates were as follows:

| Monday 8 June | Last day to file nomination papers |
| Wednesday 17 June | Campaigning officially ends |
| Thursday 18 June | Polling day |
| Friday 19 June | The Conservative Party wins power with a majority of 31 |
| Monday 29 June | [45th Parliament](/wiki/List_of_MPs_elected_in_the_1970_United_Kingdom_general_election "List of MPs elected in the 1970 United Kingdom general election") assembles |
| Thursday 2 July | [State Opening of Parliament](/wiki/State_Opening_of_Parliament "State Opening of Parliament") |

## Results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1970_United_Kingdom_general_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Results")]

This was the first general election where 18-year-olds had the right to vote. Therefore, despite 1.1 million more people voting in 1970 compared to 1966, turnout actually fell by 3%. This 72% turnout was the lowest since [the 1935 general election](/wiki/1935_United_Kingdom_general_election "1935 United Kingdom general election") and compared with a post-War high of 84% in [1950](/wiki/1950_United_Kingdom_general_election "1950 United Kingdom general election"). [Professor Richard Rose](/wiki/Richard_Rose_(political_scientist) "Richard Rose (political scientist)") described the low turnout, which he noted was "one of the lowest since the introduction of the democratic franchise", as surprising to politician and pollsters. Changes to electoral law as part of the [Representation of the People Act 1969](/wiki/Representation_of_the_People_Act_1969 "Representation of the People Act 1969") had made postal voting easier and polling stations were open an hour later than in past elections, and this would have been expected to improve turnout. On top of this it was reported by Rose that an estimated 25% of 18- to 21-year-olds who were now eligible to vote had not put their names on the electoral register, meaning the turnout was even lower than the percentage figure suggested. Rose also argued that the turnout figures in Britain were "now among the lowest in the Western world."[[2]](#cite_note-RoseTimes-3) Because the previous election had been in 1966, some people had not had their chance to vote in a general election until the age of 25. Labour's number of votes, 12.2 million, was ironically the same amount they had needed to win in 1964. The Conservative vote surge cost Labour in many marginal seats. Rose suggested the absolute fall in the number of Labour votes suggested that many of the party's supporters had decided to abstain. He also noted that the Labour Party's local organisation was poorer than that of the Conservatives, but did not feel this was a significant factor in Labour supporters failing to come out to vote for the Party given that this organisational difference had been the case in past elections without having this effect.[[2]](#cite_note-RoseTimes-3) For the Liberals, a small 1% drop in their vote share saw them lose 6 seats, 3 of which were held by the narrowest of margins.

In the end the Conservatives achieved a swing of 4.7%, enough to give them a comfortable working majority. As for the smaller parties, they increased their number in the Commons from 2 to 6 seats.

The [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") won its first ever seat at a general election (they had won several by-elections previously, going back as far as [1945](/wiki/1945_Motherwell_by-election "1945 Motherwell by-election")), although they did lose Hamilton, which they won in a by-election in 1967.
